coredata.dc:description: Herein, ginger-derived N-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) using pyrolysis method are decorated over in-situ fabricated n/n homojunction i.e., S-g-C3N4/g-C3N4 (SgCN/gCN) by hydrothermal approach for photocatalytic breakdown of rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The obtained N-C-dots exhibited crystalline nature having quasi spherical particle with sizes in 2.36–8.51 nm range, whereas SgCN/gCN has a layered morphology. The resulting ternary composite N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN demonstrated significantly greater photocatalytic efficacy than the individual components under UV-light. Under optimal circumstances i.e., pH = 3, dye concentration 5 ppm, photocatalyst dosage 0.06 g, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN displayed excellent photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye (99.68 %) in only 10 min exposure to UV-irradiations. Capture experiments revealed that O2 • − radical ions are primary reactive components for dye degradation while •OH and h+ have relatively minor contribution. The findings of capture studies were employed to propose probable photocatalytic dye decomposition route. Additionally, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN showed excellent stability and good reusability up to 5 cycles with just a small drop in photocatalyst efficiency.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, ginger-derived N-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) using pyrolysis method are decorated over in-situ fabricated n/n homojunction i.e., S-g-C3N4/g-C3N4 (SgCN/gCN) by hydrothermal approach for photocatalytic breakdown of rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The obtained N-C-dots exhibited crystalline nature having quasi spherical particle with sizes in 2.36–8.51 nm range, whereas SgCN/gCN has a layered morphology. The resulting ternary composite N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN demonstrated significantly greater photocatalytic efficacy than the individual components under UV-light. Under optimal circumstances i.e., pH = 3, dye concentration 5 ppm, photocatalyst dosage 0.06 g, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN displayed excellent photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye (99.68 %) in only 10 min exposure to UV-irradiations. Capture experiments revealed that O2 • − radical ions are primary reactive components for dye degradation while •OH and h+ have relatively minor contribution. The findings of capture studies were employed to propose probable photocatalytic dye decomposition route. Additionally, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN showed excellent stability and good reusability up to 5 cycles with just a small drop in photocatalyst efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Globally expanding industrialization has raised the serious concerns of water pollution. Because different toxic contaminants from various industries are dumped into natural water resources without any pretreatment which have extremely adverse impacts on the natural water quality [1]. Two million tonnes of effluent and other industrial or agricultural garbage are dumped into water bodies each year, endangering several thousand organisms and seriously harming marine life and human health [2]. Therefore, proper management is required for the waste products particularly industrial waste, which are composed of pharmaceuticals, heavy metals, and particularly synthetic dyes since they are utilized extensively in cosmetic, paper, textiles and printing sectors. These dyes possessing intricate structures and extended stability have the potential to inflict significant harm, including mutagenesis and carcinogenic effects in both humans and animals [3]. As a result, disposing these dyes to water resources alters not only its color but also its oxygen content. For this reason, hazardous dyes must be eliminated before introducing waste materials to natural water resources. Several investigations were performed to address the issues of the breakdown of organic dyes in industrial wastewater employing a variety of techniques, including adsorption, membrane filtering, and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) [4,5]. But AOP is an appealing and efficient process for treating industrial wastewater, especially textile waste that contains hazardous dyes [6,7]. These approaches are used in conjunction with other strategies including photocatalytic approach, which is used to decompose organic contaminants and hazardous substances that are present in water resources.

Photocatalytic treatment of wastewater is an extensively utilized eco-friendly approach that effectively decompose harmful contaminants into less toxic or non-toxic compounds [8,9]. Nevertheless, remediation of dye contaminated wastewater via photocatalysis also suffers from some limitations because the photocatalytic technique's effectiveness is dependent on a number of factors, including the kind of catalyst, the light's wavelength, catalyst's optical band-gap, etc. [10]. Consequently, type of photocatalyst employed for photocatalytic eradication of dye plays a significant role. Various different kinds of photocatalysts have been investigated for photocatalytic decontamination of wastewater including metal sulfides, oxides, carbon-based photocatalysts, etc. [11]. But in the recent past, graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) has been extensively studied in several disciplines particularly photocatalytic decomposition of organic contaminants [12]. It is a metal free n-type semiconductor having bandgap of 2.7 eV and exists in polymeric form with layered framework. Its remarkable physical and chemical characteristics, including chemically stability, light weight, biocompatibility, and resistant to water, render it perfect for photocatalytic dye degradation [13]. Nonetheless, photocatalytic results on sole gCN are restricted owing to its low performance in the visible range as a result of quick recombination of charge carriers [14].

Various strategies were employed to boost the photocatalytic efficacy of gCN. One of the effective strategies is to integrate gCN with carbon-dots (C-dots) derived from biomass [15–17]. C-dots are zero-dimensional nanomaterials (NMs) majorly consisted of C, H, O, N and some other heteroatoms. They exhibit excellent characteristics like large surface area to volume ratio, numerous surface functional moieties, low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, electron-accepting and transporting potential, up- and down conversion photoluminescence [18]. In addition, fabrication of C-dots utilizing biomass is of significant importance since the environmental contamination originated by openly dumping or burning biomass can not only be prevented but that biomass can be turned into high-valued NMs i.e., C-dots for several uses [19]. The coupling of gCN with C-dots can produce synergetic impacts such as photosensitization, electron mediation and acceptor, and increased light adsorption. Consequently, C-dots/gCN composite can take the benefits of numerous surface functional groups and electron accepting (mediator) nature of C-dots as well as vast surface area of gCN. In addition, decoration of C-dots over gCN overcome the limitation of high aqueous solubility of C-dots and fast recombination rate of charge carriers of gCN [20]. Therefore, the faster recombination rate of electron-holes couples can be reduced by integration of gCN with C-dots and hence its photocatalytic efficiency can be enhanced [21]. Fewer studies are being conducted on the photocatalytic processing of dye contaminated wastewater utilizing gCN composite with biomass derived C-dots. For example, Hak et al. demonstrated photocatalytic elimination of 2,4 dichlorophenol using carbon-quantum dots (CQDs) produced from water hyacinth and gCN composites in the presence of sun light [22].

Therefore, we have studied the impact of decoration of ginger derived N-doped C-dots over n/n-homojunction i.e., SgCN/gCN for photocatalytic treatment of aqueous RhB dye. N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN exhibited enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for RhB decontamination as compared to pristine N-C-dots and SgCN/gCN under same experimental circumstances. It is due to the fact that decoration of N-doped C-dots over SgCN/gCN reduced the electron-hole recombination rate. As a result, electrons and holes get longer time in the reaction mixture and hence lead to generation of greater number of reactive radicals for dye degradation. Furthermore, various reaction parameters affecting photocatalytic RhB degradation like concentration, pH of dye solution and photocatalyst dosage have been optimized. Under optimum conditions (pH = 3, dye concentration 5 ppm, photocatalyst dosage 0.06 g), the ternary composite i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN degraded 99.68 % RhB dye within 10 min of UV-radiations. Additionally, capture experiments, reusability and stability studies were carried out to determine the role of various radicals in dye degradation, reuse capability and stability against light-irradiations of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. Based on the acquired data from trapping studies, it was found that O2 • −, •OH and h+ radicals ions are involved in RhB dye degradation and hence these results have been used to propose the mechanism involved in photocatalytic RhB dye decomposition.

All the chemicals employed are of analytical grade such as liq. NH3 (SRL, 30 %), Na2EDTA (CDH, 99.5 %), tert-butanol (CDH, 99.5 %), urea and thiourea (CDH, 99.0 %), and para-benzoquinone (Loba Chemie, 98.0 %).

Ginger (Zingiber officinale) has been utilized as biomass precursor for eco-friendly fabrication of C-dots. It is first cleaned several times with double-distilled water, subsequently cut into small pieces and left to dry under the sun warmth for around 48 h. Following that, it has been pyrolyzed in a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 180 min. The material resulted from pyrolysis is then finely powdered utilizing a grinder. Afterwards, 800 mL of double deionized water is used for dispersing 55 g of fine powder. The aqueous mixture so obtained has been stirred for 24 h at 1200 rpm. Following this, The mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 1 h to separate bigger particles and thereafter passed through Whatman filter paper. Thereafter, adding 10 mL of liquid NH3 (dopant) to the obtained mixture, it was stirred for further 24 h. After being left undisturbed for the next 24 h, the resulting filtrate was further filtered through a syringe filter (pore size of 0.22 μm). The supernatant so obtained was heated on hot plate to completely remove water content. Finally, N-C-dots have been collected and finely powdered [23].

SgCN/gCN was synthesized in-situ using a conventional thermal pyrolysis process. After thoroughly mixing 20 g of thiourea and 20 g of urea manually in a beaker for 20 min, the mixture was transferred in 4 ceramic crucibles, each of which was tightly covered with multiple aluminum sheets. Subsequently, crucibles have been put in a muffle furnace for thermal pyrolysis of the contents for 4 h at 540 °C at a heating rate of 4 oC/min. As a result, NH3 gas was released as thiourea and urea molecules undergo self-polymerization. Throughout the thermal polycondensation procedure, sulphur atoms were assimilated right away into the g-C3N4 structure. The furnace was left to naturally cool down and subsequently the resultant SgCN/gCN sample was collected and thoroughly powdered [20].

0.04 g of N-C-dots and 1.96 g of SgCN/gCN (i.e., 2 % wt. ratio of N-C-dots) have been added into 50 mL of double DI water and subsequently stirred for 12 h to form their homogeneous solution. Afterwards, the resulting solution was put into a 110 mL autoclave and subjected to heating for 4 h at 140 °C in hot air oven for hydrothermal treatment. It led to the fabrication of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN ternary composite. Subsequently, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN containing aqueous solution has been dried at 70 °C for 12 h in hot air oven. Finally, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN was obtained and finely powdered [15].

Employing a "Rigaku Ultima IV X-Ray Diffractometer" (XRD) with CuK as X-ray source, the structural characteristics of the fabricated NMs were investigated. Functional groups in 4000–400 cm−1 range and chemical composition were examined employing FTIR and X-rays Photon Spectrophotometer, respectively. Thermo Scientific "high-resolution transmission electron microscope" (HR-TEM) has been employed to evaluate morphological characteristics. The synthesized C-dots' % purity was assessed utilizing the "STR 500 Confocal Micro Raman Spectrometer." The optical as well as photocatalytic activity of the fabricated NMs was examined using “BIOGENIX UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Model: BS-UVD-2900)”. The photoluminescence (PL) studies were carried out utilizing "HORIBA Fluoromax Spectrofluorometer".

The photocatalytic potentials of the fabricated NMs were examined by photocatalytic decomposition of aqueous RhB dye solution under a 300 W UV-radiations. Dye solutions with preset concentrations were prepared in double distilled water. Afterwards, the fixed quantities of produced NMs were dispersed in dye solutions. The resulting solutions were initially stirred for 30 min in dark to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium state. The dye solutions were then subjected to UV light. 3 mL dye solutions were regularly taken out, firstly centrifuged and then used to record UV–vis absorbance spectra for further investigations as shown in Scheme 1 . RhB dye's maximum absorption intensity has been detected in UV–vis absorption spectra at 554 nm [23].

The XRD patterns of the fabricated NMs i.e., N-C-dots, SgCN/gCN and N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN has been displayed in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of N-C-dots demonstrated its crystalline structure with some major peaks at 2θ values of 28.46o, 30.94o, 40.46o, 50.26o, 58.64o, 66.53o, and 73.80o which are associated with (002), (111), (100), (102), (600), (220) and (215) planes, sequentially [15,23]. In-situ produced SgCN/gCN exhibited characteristics XRD peaks at 12.92o and 27.50o which are linked to (110) and (002) plane, sequentially [15,24]. The ternary composite N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN exhibited XRD peaks corresponding to both of N-C-dots and SgCN/gCN.

However, the intensities of XRD peaks of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN corresponding to N-C-dots are very less and only one peak located at 2θ value of 40.46o is clearly visible in ternary composite. The XRD peak of N-C-dots located at 2θ values of 28.46o got merged with relatively broad SgCN/gCN XRD peak centered at 27.50o and hence could not be distinguished.

As displayed in Fig. 2 , FTIR examination has been done to identify different functional moieties in obtained NMs. The peaks in FTIR spectra of N-C-dots located at 3415, 2930, 1598, 1384, 1125, 1022, 775, and 618 cm−1 were assigned to O-H/N-H, C-H, C=C/N-H, CN, asymmetric C-O-C, symmetric C-O-C, various types of C-H bonds, and aromatic CH out of the plane deformation, correspondingly, clearly showed that N-C-dots were composed of a variety of functional groups [15,23,25–27]. The distinctive peaks of gCN were displayed by the in-situ fabricated SgCN/gCN.

The existence of O-H, heptazine heterocyclic (C6N7) grouping, N-H distortion, and breathing forms of triazine (heterocyclic condensed CN) of SgCN is accountable for the FTIR absorption peaks at 3283-3164, 1648-1214, 895, and 813 cm−1, respectively [20,28]. The FTIR spectra of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN is dominated by existence of various functional groups of SgCN/gCN because of its 98 % proportion in the ternary composite. However, two FTIR peaks of N-C-dots around 1123 and 621 cm−1 for asymmetric C-O-C and aromatic C-H groups, sequentially, in FTIR spectra of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN revealed the successful fabrication of ternary composite.

Several XPS spectra of fabricated NMs have been displayed in Fig. 3 and 4
. As displayed in Fig. 3 (a), N-C-dots composed of C, N and O elements. Its C1s spectra is composed of 4 peaks at 282.10, 285.46, 289.94 and 292.73 eV were assigned to C-H [29], C=C/CN [30–32], C=O/HO-C=O [33,34] and π-electrons’ movement [32], correspondingly (Fig. 3 (b)), whereas 2 peaks linked to C=O [35] and C-OH [30] are detected in O1s XPS spectra at 528.21 and 529.48 eV, sequentially (Fig. 3 (c)). Furthermore, the C-dots’ N1s spectra is deconvoluted into 4 peaks centered at 397.57, 398.56, 399.41 and 400.29 eV attributed to C-N-C, pyridinic-N, N-(C3) and N-H bonds, correspondingly [15,23] (Fig. 3 (d)).

The XPS survey spectra of SgCN/gCN demonstrated the presence of C, S, and N elements as demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a). Two peaks at 285.59 and 287.86 eV in C1s spectra of SgCN/gCN were ascribed to presence of carbon impurities and N=C-N, sequentially (Fig. 4 (b)) [15]. The N1s spectra of SgCN/gCN has been deconvoluted into 2 peaks associated with C=N-C and N-(C3) groups at 396.37 and 398.67 eV, sequentially (Fig. 4 (c)) [23], whereas, its S2p spectra is comprised of 2 peaks at 165.18 and 166.61 eV linked to -C-S- and N-S groups, respectively (Fig. 4 (d)) [35,36]. Furthermore, the presence of all the elements of SgCN/gCN and N-C-dots in N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN survey spectra showed the successful decoration of N-C-dots over SgCN/gCN homojunction as displayed in Fig. 4 (e) [37].

The resulting N-C-dots showed distinctive peaks at 1362 and 1558 cm−1 in Raman spectra, as demonstrated in Fig. 5 (a). A notably sharper peak at 1558 cm−1 (known as the G-Band) is linked to the existence of a schematic framework of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. On the other flip, the peak centered at 1362 cm−1, also known as the D-band, is caused by the existence of a haphazard pattern of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in carbon dots. Based on the ratio of D- and G-bands’ intensity, the purity percentage of N-C-dots has been determined to be 90.18 % [38]. Furthermore, as displayed in Fig. 5(b), SgCN/gCN displayed the distinctive peaks of gCN, namely the D- and G-bands, which are centered at 1367 and 1550 cm−1 [15,39]. Furthermore, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN showed Raman peaks of individual NMs, i.e., N-C-dots and SgCN/gCN with minimal alteration in the peak's positions.

The morphological description of fabricated samples has been examined using HRTEM images. The histogram representing the distribution of particles size of N-C-dots, along with HRTEM images of N-C-dots and N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN have been shown in Fig. 6 . The N-C-dots were quasi-spherical having mean size of 5.83 nm. It is evident from Fig. 6 (c) that SgCN/gCN has layered framework and N-C-dots are successfully adorned over SgCN/gCN [15].

UV–vis absorbance spectra and the corresponding Tauc's plots of N-C-dots, SgCN/gCN, and N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN have been demonstrated in Fig. 7 . The green fabricated N-C-dots showed distinct absorption peak at 300 nm, which is ascribed to the electrons' n-π∗ transition (C=O or C=N) [21,40]. SgCN/gCN, on the other hand, showed two peaks: a broad peak at 390 nm and a comparatively sharp peak at 323 nm, which were caused by n-π∗ and π-π∗ electronic movement of the conjugated aromatic s-heptazine ring, respectively [41]. It was observed that upon decoration of N-C-dots over SgCN/gCN, the band gap of SgCN/gCN homojunction got expanded from 2.93 eV to 3.14 eV which probably caused by quantum size effect and presence of structural defects in N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN [20,42]. The same observations have already been reported by Wang et al. [43] and Phang et al. [20].

Fig. 8 (a) displays the distinctive excitation wavelength (λ ex) based PL spectra of N-C-dots, together with a constant red-shifting of the peaks of PL emission spectra. The peak intensity of PL emission plots elevated with a rise in λ ex from 440 to 470 nm, and it began to decrease after λ ex of 470 nm. The occurrence of many emissive zones on the outermost regions of C-dots and variations in their particle sizes are the probable causes of this PL nature of N-C-dots. But it is still unclear exactly what process is responsible for the λex-dependent PL spectrum of C-dots [35]. Fig. 8 (b) showed the PL spectra of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN and SgCN/gCN. It can be noticed that at λ ex = 360 nm, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN has smaller PL emission intensity in comparison to that of SgCN/gCN. Enhanced electron-hole movement and slower recombination of charge carriers were demonstrated by reduced PL emission spectrum intensity of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. It additionally indicates that ternary composite is more effective at decontaminating dyes via photocatalysis [44].

By optimizing various reaction variables, like photocatalyst dosage, dye concentration, and pH of aqueous medium, the photocatalytic efficacy of synthesized NMs have been investigated through photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of distinct synthesized samples on photocatalytic decomposition of RhB
Impact of different dosages of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN
ce:section.ce:para: Studies were carried out utilizing various photocatalyst doses (0.04–0.07 g) to examine the effect of photocatalyst dosage on RhB removal. To assess the influence of photocatalyst amount on dye breakdown, 0.04–0.07 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN and 50 mL of RhB dye (5 ppm) were used, as demonstrated in Fig. 10 (a) and listed in Table 2 . As the dose of the photocatalyst was increased, the extent of dye breakdown was enhanced and reached the optimum level at 0.06 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. Following this the proportion of dye degradation decreased. It should be emphasized that 0.06 g of ternary composite exhibited the highest deterioration of RhB dye in only 20 min. Consequently, 20 min study were conducted to determine impact of photocatalyst dosages to decontaminate RhB dye.

The efficiency of the photocatalyst in catalyzing processes is dependent on the number of active regions present on its surface. Increment in photocatalyst dosage frequently resulted into enhancement in the overall surface area and number of surface functional groups, which ultimately results in higher extent of dye degradation. It's important to note that increasing the dose of photocatalyst from 0.06 to 0.07 g resulted in a drop in the extent of dye removal. This impact results from a decline in absorption of UV light caused by rise in photocatalyst quantity, which in turn causes a drop in the degree of photoactivated volume of solution [11,19].

Different RhB dye solutions having conc. of 5, 10, 15, and 20 ppm were prepared to investigate the effect of dye content. For the RhB dye decontamination, 50 mL of all dye solutions were exposed to UV irradiations for 20 min utilizing 0.06 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. With increment in dye content, the proportion of dye degradation declined, as summarized in Table 3 . As demonstrated in Fig. 10(b), the highest decontamination of RhB dye occurred at conc. of 5 ppm. It is due to the fact that the capability of the photocatalyst to degrade dyes is closely linked to the formation of e−-h+ couple and, consequently, the generation of strongly reactive radicals on its outermost layer, such as O2 • − and •OH. Additionally, when the concentration of dye rises, the incoming photons' penetration length shortens and, therefore, the photocatalyst's capability for absorbing photons is lowered, causing a drop in photocatalytic effectiveness for dye breakdown [23,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pH of dye solution and determination of pHpzc of hybrid nanojunction
ce:section.ce:para: The most effective pH of aqueous medium for photocatalytic dye decomposition may be found from the photocatalyst's point of zero charges (pHpzc). The photocatalyst's surface attains positive charge if aqueous dye medium's pH drops below pHpzc of the photocatalysts, and vice versa [46]. Fig. 11 (a) illustrates the pHpzc of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN, which was calculated to be 2.47 by employing the salt addition approach [23]. This implies that N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN surface acquires positive charge at pH values of dye solution lower than 2.47. In such a situation, lesser photocatalytic dye breakdown results from the electrostatic repulsive forces pulling apart the positively charged N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN and cationic dye. Consequently, for promoting the photocatalytic breakdown of the cationic RhB dye, the N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN surface must be appropriately anionic, as this enhances the electrostatic attractive force between N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN and the dye molecules.

The current study investigated the impact of dye solution pH on photocatalytic dye decontamination using 50 mL of 5 ppm RhB dye solution at various pH levels (1.75–7) using 0.06 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. As demonstrated in Fig. 11(b) and summed up in Table 4 , it was found that the extent of dye decomposition increased first with increase in pH of dye, approached its highest level at pH 3, and then decreased [47]. It implies that at pH 3 of dye solution, the rate of RhB degradation reached its maximum and the time needed for its maximum photocatalytic degradation decreased from 20 to 10 min. But, the photocatalyst's effectiveness keeps on decreasing with increase in pH of RhB dye solutions beyond pH 3. At higher pH of dye solution, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN efficacy is drastically decreased by the formation of a thin film on its surface, which is probably resulting from cationic RhB dye molecules adsorbed more extensively on the highly negatively charged N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN surface. This film prevents irradiated UV photons from reaching the N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN surface at higher pH levels, which slows down the dye's degradation [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of reaction kinetics of photocatalytic RhB dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the most efficient sample i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN, the photocatalytic decomposition of RhB dye has been shown in Fig. 12 . The absorption peak's intensity at 554 nm for RhB dye decreased with irradiation duration indicating the reduction in dye content. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 12(b), RhB dye showed very little degradation whether exposed to light solely as well as under dark circumstances.

As demonstrated in Fig. 13 , the linearly fitted plot of ln(Ao/At) with irradiation time revealed the first-order kinetics of photocatalytic dye degradation. First-order kinetics is additionally confirmed by correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.98956) value. The R2 values for the linear fitted plot of ln(Ao/At) against time for the first-order reactions range from 0.95 to 1 (Table 5 ) [49]. The following formulas are used to study first-order reactions and its half-life [15,23]:

The price for electricity is 4.68 (INR) for each unit in our area, with a 500-unit highest permissible consumption. Increased photocatalyst economic viability and decreased total electrical costs for dye breakdown are two benefits of enhanced dye elimination capability. The synthesized NMs' photocatalytic efficacy is N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN > SgCN/gCN > N-C-dots, as previously stated. Therefore, the N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN hybrid nanocomposite is the most affordable and effective photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability studies of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN
ce:section.ce:para: Investigations have been done to ascertain the financial affordability and long-term sustainability of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. As can be seen in Fig. 14 (a) , N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN exhibited an amazing reusable feature up to 5 rounds with only a small decrement in its efficiency for dye decomposition; it is most probably because the weight of photocatalyst reduced with each subsequent cycle during the process of recovering [51]. Additionally, XRD analysis of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN was done before and after five photocatalytic cycles to assess its stability. The XRD pattern of fresh and reused samples are shown in Fig. 14 (b). The freshly fabricated and recycled sample's XRD pattern showed minor deviations showing remarkable stability of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN [52,53]. Furthermore, Table 7 clearly shows that the synthesized N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN composite utilized in this study has a greater photocatalytic RhB dye decomposition efficacy compared to those described in other articles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed reaction pathway of RhB photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN is a Type-II heterojunction photocatalyst [58]. Here the CB and VB of N-C-dots are both higher than the corresponding bands of semiconductor SgCN/gCN. As a result, photogenerated electrons and holes migrate and are confined in opposite directions in semiconductors SgCN/gCN and N-C-dots, respectively, driven by the CB and VB offsets, as illustrated in Fig. 15 [59]. Photocatalytic removal process of RhB dye is initiated when UV-rays having sufficient energy incident on N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN. Following UV-radiations incident, electrons from VB of SgCN/gCN and N-C-dots get excited to CB with equal number of h+ to form photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Then, due to the interlaced side band position of SgCN/gCN and N-C-dots, e− migrated from CB of N-C-dots to CB of SgCN/gCN and reacted with O2 to generate O2 • − radical. The h+ in VB of SgCN/gCN was transferred to VB of N-C-dots, and h+ could also oxidize RhB to produce oxidation products such as CO2 and H2O [60]. The doped C-dots have two primary roles in N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN: 1) they capture the electrons and prevent the reassembling of e− and h+ of SgCN/gCN, and thus facilitate the quicker movement of charge carriers for the required photocatalytic processes, 2) the surface functional components of photocatalyst such as -COOH, -OH, -COH, -CONH2, etc., facilitate the adsorption of dye molecules on photocatalyst surface through H-bond connections [15]. Consequently, the photo-produced e−/h+ couples facilitate the generation of extremely reactive radicals for photocatalytic dye decontamination, including •OH and O2 •−radicals [61]. Equations (7)–(20) show the usual chemical routeway that occurs in the course of photocatalytic decomposition of dye by the photocatalyst (Fig. 15) [2,16,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of distinct trapping agents on RhB photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: A photocatalyst when subjected to light irradiations in an aqueous medium typically produces holes, O2 • −, and •OH radicals as main photo-reactive radicals [63]. These radicals effectively assist in dye treatment through photocatalysis. To fully understand the function of these radicals in the photocatalytic processing of dye-contaminated wastewater, experiments on the trapping of these radicals were conducted. Three entrapment tests were conducted utilizing 5 mL of 10 mM of p-BZQ, tertiary-butyl alcohol (t-BA), along with Na2EDTA for O2 • −, •OH, and h+ trapping, correspondingly. It was noticed that p-BZQ greatly inhibited RhB photocatalytic degradation by trapping O2 • −, while t-BA and Na2EDTA reduced RhB photocatalytic degradation to a smaller degree by trapping •OH and h+ in the dye solution (Fig. 16 ). The % photocatalytic dye decomposition decreased to 19.53 %, 76.23 %, and 84.58 % for RhB dye, correspondingly, with the inclusion of p-BZQ, t-BA, and Na2EDTA in dye solution. This suggests that while •OH and h+ play only a minor role in photocatalytic RhB breakdown, O2 • − performs the predominant contribution. Therefore, O2 • − > •OH > h+ is the order in which these radicals contribute in RhB breakdown [64].

N-C-dots synthesized from ginger by pyrolysis technique were decorated over SgCN/gCN homojunction employing hydrothermal method. Contrarily to N-C-dots which exhibited quasi-spherical nature, n/n homojunction SgCN/gCN had layered framework. The fabricated NMs have been assessed for photocatalytic decomposition of aqueous RhB dye and all the related parameters like % degradation, half-life, rate constant, electricity consumption and cost have been determined. In addition, various variables, such as photocatalyst amount, pH of aqueous medium and dye conc. have been optimized. Under optimal conditions (0.06 g photocatalyst amount, 5 ppm dye conc., and pH = 3), the N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN photocatalyst exhibited 99.68 % RhB degradation in just 10 min of UV-irradiations. An improvement in the photocatalytic efficiency of N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN is attributed to broadening of band gap upon decoration of C-dots over SgCN/gCN. Additionally, PL analysis showed that N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN exhibited a decreased electron-hole reunification rate and a substantially quicker-charged particle mobility, which leads to higher degree of dye decomposition. The capture studies revealed that O2 • − is the most prevalent radical in dye degradation, while h+ and •OH radicals had a less important role. Moreover, N-C-dots/SgCN/gCN exhibited excellent stability and recyclability over five rounds despite only a small reduction in dye removal efficiency. This investigation indicated that decorating N-C-dots over SgCN/gCN provides an effective approach for the successful RhB dye degradation.
